questions.question: Explain Sequential Access method and state its advantages for batch processing applications.
keyPoints: Sequential access reads/writes records in consecutive order
Head must move sequentially through file
Very efficient for batch processing where entire file is processed
Simple to implement with minimal overhead
Ideal for operations like generating reports, backups, and data sorting
Minimizes seek operations when accessing consecutive records

questions.question: What is the difference between Sequential Access and Direct Access methods? Give one suitable use case for each.
keyPoints: Sequential: reads records in order, must traverse preceding records
Direct/Random: can access any record directly without reading others
Sequential suitable for: payroll processing, log file analysis, batch operations
Direct suitable for: database systems, airline reservation systems, ATM transactions
Direct access uses formula: address = starting address + (record number × record size)

questions.question: Describe the Indexed Access method and explain how it achieves better performance than linked allocation for random access.
keyPoints: Indexed access uses an index block containing pointers to all data blocks
Index block acts as lookup table for data block addresses
Can directly access any block by first reading index block
Linked allocation requires traversing entire chain for random access
Indexed allocation supports both efficient random and sequential access
Requires extra disk space for index blocks

questions.question: Explain Contiguous Allocation and state two advantages and two disadvantages of this method.
keyPoints: Contiguous allocation stores each file in consecutive disk blocks
Advantages: 1) Supports fast sequential and direct access 2) Simple to implement - address calculation is straightforward
Disadvantages: 1) Suffers from external fragmentation 2) Difficult to expand files - may require relocation
Despite disadvantages, provides best performance for certain workloads

questions.question: What is the difference between External Fragmentation and Internal Fragmentation? Which allocation methods are affected by each?
keyPoints: External fragmentation: free space scattered throughout disk, preventing large file allocation despite sufficient total space
Internal fragmentation: wasted space within allocated units due to fixed block sizes
Contiguous allocation suffers most from external fragmentation
Linked and indexed allocation minimize external fragmentation
All fixed-block allocation methods suffer from some internal fragmentation
Internal fragmentation is considered less problematic than external fragmentation

questions.question: Compare Linked Allocation and Indexed Allocation of files on disk.
keyPoints: Linked: stores file as chain of blocks with pointers to next block
Indexed: uses index block containing pointers to all data blocks
Linked only supports sequential access efficiently
Indexed supports both sequential and direct access efficiently
Linked has no external fragmentation but loses space for pointers
Indexed requires extra space for index block but no fragmentation
Indexed is generally preferred for modern file systems

questions.question: Define Relative Access method. How does it convert relative record numbers to physical disk addresses?
keyPoints: Relative access assigns a relative number (0 to n-1) to each record
User specifies record number, system calculates physical address
Formula: physical address = starting address + (relative record number × record size)
Provides direct access capability without complex indexing
Simpler than indexed access but requires fixed record sizes
Commonly used in older mainframe systems

questions.question: Discuss in detail the three main file allocation methods: Contiguous, Linked, and Indexed. Compare their performance for sequential and random access operations.
keyPoints: CONTIGUOUS: Stores files in consecutive blocks. Sequential: O(1) for consecutive blocks. Random: O(1) using formula. Problems: external fragmentation, file growth difficulty
LINKED: Files stored as linked list of blocks. Sequential: O(1) for next block. Random: O(n) must traverse chain. Advantages: no external fragmentation. Disadvantage: no true random access

INDEXED: Uses index block with pointers. Sequential: O(1) for index + O(1) for data. Random: O(1) for index + O(1) for data. Advantages: supports both access types well. Disadvantage: extra index block needed
Performance comparison: Contiguous best for both when file size fixed. Linked poor for random. Indexed good for both but uses extra space
Modern systems often use variations like multi-level indexing, FAT systems, or inode-based indexed allocation

questions.question: Explain the various factors that affect disk access time. How do different access methods optimize for these factors?
keyPoints: Disk access time components: 1) Seek time - moving head to correct track (largest component), 2) Rotational latency - waiting for sector to rotate under head, 3) Transfer time - actual data transfer
Sequential access optimization: minimizes seeks by reading consecutive blocks, takes advantage of prefetching and caching
Direct access optimization: calculates exact location to minimize unnecessary seeks, but may cause random seeks across disk
Indexed access optimization: uses index to directly locate blocks reducing search time, but requires extra read for index
Contiguous allocation: best for sequential and direct access due to minimal seek operations
Linked allocation: good for sequential but poor for random due to potential long seek chains
Choosing access method depends on access patterns: sequential-heavy vs random-heavy workloads

questions.question: Design a file system scenario where Sequential Access would be more appropriate than Direct Access, and another where Direct Access would be preferred. Justify your choices.
keyPoints: Scenario 1 - Sequential Access: Monthly payroll processing system

Justification: All employee records processed in order each month, no random record lookups needed, entire file read sequentially, sequential access is fastest for this pattern, minimizes disk seeks and rotational delays, batch processing dominates
Scenario 2 - Direct Access: Banking ATM transaction system

Justification: Each transaction accesses specific customer account randomly, need immediate access to any account, transactions are independent and unpredictable, cannot wait to traverse sequential records, direct/indexed access essential for response time requirements
Key principle: Access method should match the access pattern of the application
Mismatch leads to poor performance: sequential access for random workload wastes time, direct access for sequential workload causes unnecessary seeks

questions.question: Explain the working of an indexed file allocation system. How does it handle large files using multi-level indexing? What are its advantages and disadvantages?
keyPoints: Basic indexed allocation: Single index block per file contains pointers to all data blocks
For access: Read index block, find pointer to desired data block, read data block - two I/O operations per access
Handling large files: Single-level index has limited capacity (e.g., with 4KB block and 4-byte pointers, max ~1000 blocks)
Multi-level indexing: First-level index points to second-level indexes, which point to data blocks
Example: Two-level with 1024 pointers per level can address 1024 × 1024 = 1 million blocks
Advantages: Supports efficient direct and sequential access, no external fragmentation, good for large files with multi-level
Disadvantages: Extra disk space for index blocks, additional I/O for index reads (especially multi-level), index block may become single point of failure
Used in UNIX/Linux inodes, NTFS, and other modern file systems
